title: Tensor.item
text: Tensor.item() returns the value of a tensor as a standard Python number. This method only works when the tensor has exactly one element. If the tensor has more than one element, .item() raises an error because the tensor cannot be converted to a single scalar value. For example, if x = torch.tensor(3.14), then x.item() returns the Python float 3.14. If x = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0]), then x.item() raises an error because the tensor has two elements. This method is commonly used to extract scalar loss values for logging during training: loss_value = loss.item(). It converts the single-element tensor to a Python scalar, which is useful for printing, storing in lists, or passing to non-PyTorch code that expects a plain number rather than a tensor. For tensors with more than one element, use .tolist() to convert the entire tensor to a nested Python list, or index the tensor to select a specific element before calling .item(). For example, x[0].item() extracts the first element as a scalar. The returned type matches the tensor's dtype: a float32 tensor returns a Python float, an int64 tensor returns a Python int, and a bool tensor returns a Python bool.

title: torch.argmax
text: torch.argmax(input, dim=None, keepdim=False) returns the indices of the maximum values along a dimension. It does not return the maximum values themselves, only their positions. For a 2D tensor, torch.argmax(x, dim=1) returns a 1D tensor of integer indices, one per row, indicating the column position of the largest element in each row. When called without a dim argument, torch.argmax(x) returns the index of the overall maximum in the flattened tensor as a scalar tensor. The output dtype is always torch.int64 regardless of the input dtype. If you need the maximum values instead of indices, torch.argmax is the wrong choice. Use torch.max(input, dim) instead, which returns both the maximum values and their indices as a named tuple. The distinction is important: indices tell you where the maximum is, values tell you what the maximum is. torch.argmax is commonly used in classification tasks to convert model logits into predicted class labels: predicted_classes = torch.argmax(logits, dim=1). It is also used to find the position of the best score in attention mechanisms or beam search. For cases where multiple elements share the maximum value, argmax returns the index of the first occurrence.

title: torch.max
text: torch.max has two main calling conventions. When called with just a tensor, torch.max(input) returns the single maximum value across all elements as a scalar tensor. When called with a tensor and a dimension, torch.max(input, dim) returns a named tuple of (values, indices), where values contains the maximum values along that dimension and indices contains the positions of those maximum values. For example, values, indices = torch.max(x, dim=1) gives both the actual maximum values per row and the column positions where they occur. This is the key difference from torch.argmax, which only returns indices without the values. Use torch.max when you need the actual maximum values, and use torch.argmax when you only need the positions. The values tensor has the same dtype as the input. The indices tensor has dtype torch.int64. When keepdim=True is specified, the output tensors retain the reduced dimension with size 1, which is useful for broadcasting in subsequent operations. torch.max can also compare two tensors element-wise: torch.max(a, b) returns the element-wise maximum, similar to torch.maximum. For reduction operations, torch.amax is an alternative that supports reducing over multiple dimensions at once.
